name: Zamora city tour — circular from the sports city by the Castle and the Walls of Zamora
name_original: Zamora city tour — circular desde la ciudad deportiva por el Castillo y las Murallas de Zamora
region_intro: Hiking around Zamora offers diverse natural landscapes, from the glacial lakes and ancient forests of Lago de Sanabria Natural Park to the deep canyons of Arribes del Duero Natural Park. The province features extensive wetlands at Lagunas de Villafáfila and gentle hills in the Sierra de la Culebra. This varied terrain provides a range of hiking opportunities across different ecosystems. Best hiking trails around Zamora The most popular hiking route is Zamora city tour — circular from the sports city by the Castle and the Walls of Zamora, a 2.9 miles (4.6 km) trail that takes 1 hour 15 minutes to complete. This route explores the city's historic core, including its castle and ancient walls. Another top favourite among local hikers is Aceñas de Olivares from Playa de los Pelambres – circular by the Río Duero, an easy 4.0 miles (6.5 km) path. This trail follows the Río Duero, offering views of the river and the historic Aceñas de Olivares. Local hikers also love the Bosque de Valorio y Aceñas de Olivares — circular in Zamora via the Puente de Piedra, a 4.5 miles (7.3 km) trail leading through Bosque de Valorio and past the Puente de Piedra, often completed in about 1 hour 54 minutes. Hiking around Zamora is defined by glacial lakes, deep river canyons, and ancient forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy city walks to more challenging routes in natural parks. The routes in Zamora are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 180 hikers have used komoot to explore Zamora's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Zamora?
A: Zamora offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 30 routes available on komoot. These range from easy city walks to more challenging trails in natural parks, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Zamora?
A: Zamora's hiking trails traverse diverse landscapes. You can explore the glacial lakes and ancient forests of Lago de Sanabria Natural Park, the dramatic canyons of Arribes del Duero Natural Park, extensive wetlands at Lagunas de Villafáfila, and the gentle hills of the Sierra de la Culebra. The region also features river valleys, historic areas, and local forests like the Valorio Forest.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Zamora?
A: Yes, Zamora has many easy hiking options. Approximately 18 of the routes are classified as easy. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1123961002/acenas-de-olivares-from-playa-de-los-pelambres-circular-by-the-rio-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aceñas de Olivares from Playa de los Pelambres – circular by the Río Duero&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.0-mile path along the river.

Q: Can I find challenging or difficult hikes in Zamora?
A: While the komoot guide for Zamora primarily features easy and moderate routes, the region itself offers more challenging options, particularly in areas like Lago de Sanabria Natural Park. Trails such as the Cárdena and Segundera Canyons Trail or the Trail of San Martín Cave and Tera Canyon are known for significant elevation gain and glacial landscapes.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes in Zamora?
A: Yes, many routes in Zamora are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022943980/zamora-city-tour-circular-from-the-sports-city-by-the-castle-and-the-walls-of-zamora" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zamora city tour — circular from the sports city by the Castle and the Walls of Zamora&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022932221/bosque-de-valorio-y-acenas-de-olivares-circular-in-zamora-via-the-puente-de-piedra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bosque de Valorio y Aceñas de Olivares — circular in Zamora via the Puente de Piedra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any hikes in Zamora that feature waterfalls?
A: Yes, Zamora is home to impressive waterfalls. The Arribes del Duero Natural Park features the notable Pozo de los Humos (Well of Smokes) waterfall, where water plunges over 200 meters. Closer to Zamora, you can find the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442448" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cadozos Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442560" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Palia Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which are accessible via various routes.

Q: What natural attractions can I explore on hikes around Zamora?
A: Beyond waterfalls, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5441963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valorio Forest&lt;/a&gt;, a local green space perfect for walks. The region also boasts the vast Lago de Sanabria, the largest glacial lake in the Iberian Peninsula, and the unique ancient yew trees of Bosque de Tejedelo. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442442" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ricobayo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers scenic views as well.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting while hiking in Zamora?
A: Zamora is excellent for wildlife enthusiasts. The Sierra de la Culebra is renowned for having the largest population of wolves in Western Europe, and in autumn, it's popular for observing red deer. The Lagunas de Villafáfila are a significant wetland for aquatic, steppe, and migratory birds, including greylag geese and the great bustard. Arribes del Duero is also home to the black stork.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Zamora?
A: The diverse landscapes of Zamora offer hiking opportunities year-round. Spring and autumn are generally ideal for pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, with autumn being particularly popular in Sierra de la Culebra for observing red deer. In winter, snowshoe routes are available in higher altitudes, such as around Laguna de los Peces.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Zamora?
A: Yes, many of the easier routes in Zamora are suitable for families. The city walks, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022943980/zamora-city-tour-circular-from-the-sports-city-by-the-castle-and-the-walls-of-zamora" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zamora city tour&lt;/a&gt;, are generally flat and accessible. The Valorio Forest also provides a gentle, local green space for family walks.

Q: Are dogs allowed on hiking trails in Zamora?
A: While specific regulations can vary by natural park or protected area, generally, dogs are welcome on many hiking trails in Zamora, especially if kept on a leash. It's always advisable to check local park rules for specific restrictions, particularly in wildlife-sensitive areas like Lagunas de Villafáfila or Sierra de la Culebra.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Zamora?
A: The hiking routes in Zamora are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 10 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's diverse natural beauty, from the tranquil river paths to the historic city routes, and the variety of options available for all skill levels.

The Cathedral of San Salvador in Zamora, also known as the Cathedral of the Savior, is a Romanesque jewel and an important historical monument in Spain. 
The cathedral is an outstanding example of Douro Romanesque, with Byzantine influences, especially in its famous dome.
It was built between 1140 and 1174 and is the smallest and oldest of the eleven cathedrals in the autonomous community of Castilla y León.
To visit the Cathedral of San Salvador in Zamora and learn more about its history, architecture and the works of art it houses, you can consult its official website or plan a visit to experience its beauty in person. I hope you enjoy discovering this Spanish Romanesque treasure! 🏰✨

Although it was never a palatial castle but a fortress in which to protect itself and the city, it is the element that best defines the history of Zamora, receiving, in addition, the maximum protection within the Spanish Historical Heritage in 1931.
According to the chronicles, it was ordered to be built by Alfonso II, although studies carried out by historians show that it would actually be ordered to be built by Fernando I, so the building would date from the mid-11th century, although very few remains remain from this period.
The picturesque Plaza de Viriato, in front of the historic 15th-century Parador de Zamora, is a popular haunt for locals.

The Parador de Zamora, a 15th-century palace of the Counts of Alba and Alisteau in the historic center of Zamora, has been transformed into an atmospheric hotel. The inner courtyard with fountain is beautiful, as is the view of the city from the terrace.

The Plaza Mayor of Zamora is a rectangular space. This square, typically Castilian, has a square shape and is surrounded by low-rise buildings with arcades. Its location in the historic center and its pedestrian nature make it a prominent place in the city
